text: Number two, deal with the topic here. As you see in your bulletin, the sanctity of marriage is the title for the message this evening. That word sanctity carries with it the idea of the quality of being holy, and we understand, and we'll get through the message this evening, that God is the one who established marriage, amen? And the record of Genesis 2, as we've been studying the book of Genesis, we find that this chapter, Genesis 2, concludes with a beautiful record of the first union, the marriage, if you will, between man and woman, and here, God established the plan for marriage, and we see that He is the overseer. If you'd start with me and look at verse 22, I know that the text specifically will be verses 24 and 25, but let's get what's taking place here. The Bible says in verse number 22, and the rib which the Lord God had taken from man made He a woman, and brought her unto the man. And Adam said, this is now bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh, she shall be called woman because she was taken out of man. Therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother and shall cleave unto his wife, and they shall be one flesh. They were both naked, the man and his wife, and were not ashamed. We understand that it's chapter number three where the fall of man is recorded, we'll get into that over the next several weeks, where sin entered into the world, and so we carry at the end of chapter number two that same state of innocence that we dealt with last Sunday evening as we studied Genesis chapter number two and we find life in the garden and where God had prepared this place specifically for His creation, Adam and Eve, for mankind, we find that not only did He prepare it specifically, He planted that garden in that specific place, but He put man there in the garden. And we dealt with several things last week with regard to life there in the garden and all that God would give us concerning the attributes and the beauty and what have you, but we come to the end of the chapter and we find here that God begins to share with us this union, if you will, this marriage between Adam and Eve. You'll remember from verse number 22 as we pick it up in context, that here the Bible tells us that God is the one who brought Eve to Adam. The Bible ends in verse number 22 and says, and brought her, speaking of Eve, unto the man, speaking of Adam. And I'm thankful that here God was meeting the need that Adam had. It was a known thing. God knew in His wisdom and His foreknowledge and all of His omniscience, He knew that man was not gonna be alone. He was gonna make a help meet for him. And here we find that God brings Eve to Adam. We find also in verse number 23 that Adam had a clear understanding that this which now stood before him was different than anything he had ever seen. Amen? And one of the instructions that He provided them with, according to verse number 28 of Genesis one, was to be fruitful and multiply. And here we find that God is bringing the two together. He brings Eve to Adam. And then the principle is established here in verse number 24 that we'll look at this evening when we think about the sanctity of marriage where the Bible says, therefore shall a man leave his father and his mother and shall cleave unto his wife and they shall be one flesh. As we would understand this evening, it is important for us to recognize marriage, that marriage is not the idea of man. It is not the idea of government, amen? It is God's plan. It was God that designed marriage. And as the designer, as the planner, He has the right to establish the pattern and He did here in Genesis two. And it's important that we think biblically. I often speak of that importance in our lives and my desire as a follower of Christ to think biblically and to be able to answer questions not based upon what my opinion is, what your opinion is, what the popular opinion of the society, the culture in which we live, what's gonna save my neck, so to speak, what's gonna keep a crowd. I mean, we could get into all kinds of mess if we follow that pattern, but how important it is that we think and act biblically.
